text:
Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors like $\text{BaTiO}_3$ possess a remnant polarization that can be leveraged to drive the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, we provide the first demonstration of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation using barium titanate nanocrystals. Single-crystalline nanocrystals with a ferroelectric tetragonal structure were synthesized via a hydrothermal route from $\text{TiO}_2$ and barium hydroxide with a 63% yield.

When deposited on tantalum substrates, these $\text{BaTiO}_3$ nanocrystal films achieve water oxidation photocurrents of $0.141 \text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at $1.23 \text{ V}_{\text{RHE}}$ under UV illumination ($60 \text{ mW cm}^{-2}$). By applying an electric polarization of $52.8 \text{ kV cm}^{-1}$ normal to the film plane, we could modulate the performance: the photocurrent increased by a factor of 2 or decreased by a factor of 3.5, depending on the field polarity. This polarization also induced shifts in the onset potential by $-0.15 \text{ V}$ or $+0.09 \text{ V}$ and significantly modified the surface photovoltage (SPV) response. 

Furthermore, pre-exposure to an electric field enhanced the photocatalytic $\text{H}_2$ evolution rate of $\text{Pt/BaTiO}_3$ by approximately 1.5 times and improved the facet-selective photodeposition of silver onto the (001) nanocrystal surfaces. Crucially, all FE-induced enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the samples above the Curie temperature of $\text{BaTiO}_3$. These phenomena be attributed to FE dipole-induced modifications of the potential drop across the material's space charge layer. These results highlight the potential of using the ferroelectric effect to design more efficient solar-to-fuel conversion systems.
